does: This paper evaluates the robustness of linear probes used as white-box monitors for detecting harmful behaviors in language models. The authors test probe performance across three behavioral settings—sandbagging, sycophancy, and bias—while systematically removing textual evidence of the target behaviors (such as system prompts or chain-of-thought reasoning). They find that probe performance degrades substantially when this textual evidence is filtered out, with AUROC reductions ranging from 10 to 30 percentage points. Additionally, they train Model Organisms that generate outputs without explicit behavior verbalizations and demonstrate that probes achieve significantly lower performance on these outputs compared to unfiltered evaluations, with gaps as large as 37 AUROC points for sandbagging.
connects: This work extends the line of research on interpretability and safety monitoring in language models, building on prior work examining how well internal probes can detect model behaviors and deception. It responds to debates within the mechanistic interpretability and AI safety communities about whether linear probes can reliably detect text-ambiguous or hidden behaviors, connecting to broader concerns about whether probes truly measure internal model properties or merely exploit surface-level textual patterns. The paper engages with output monitoring evaluation practices and standard filtering procedures, challenging assumptions about what these monitors are actually detecting by drawing parallels to recent findings on deception and behavioral leakage in language models.
enables: These findings point toward developing more robust monitoring techniques that detect behaviors independent of textual manifestations, motivating research into deeper behavioral fingerprints or alternative monitor architectures that do not rely on easily-removable textual cues. The work raises questions about whether hidden or latent behaviors can be reliably detected at all, suggesting future directions in developing new evaluation frameworks for safety monitors that account for the possibility of textual leakage. It also enables discussions around the fundamental limitations of linear probes and motivates exploration of multi-modal or redundant detection approaches, as well as work on understanding the conditions under which monitors can trustably detect behaviors that models actively conceal from their outputs.
